title: Valley of Flowers & Hemkund Sahib
summary: A striking dual alpine landscape: an emerald valley carpeted in hundreds of monsoon wildflower species, paired with a steep, punishing ascent to a sacred high-altitude glacial lake wrapped in mist.
story: Stepping into the Valley of Flowers feels like crossing the threshold into a living canvas. Hemmed in by massive, misty mountain walls, the valley floor transforms completely under monsoon showers, shifting its color palette weekly as rare endemic blooms compete for space.

The next dawn completely changes the tone. The trek up to Hemkund Sahib is a relentless, gravity-defying climb. Pushing through thin air at 4,632 meters, the rewards hit all at once: a completely silent crystal tarn, floating ice sheets, and the quiet devotion echoing from the world's highest Gurudwara.

gallery.caption: Deep inside the monsoon bloom of the valley
Glacial streams cutting through a sea of green
Navigating the steep switchbacks through the mist
The serene and sacred high-altitude lake front

title: Stok Kangri
summary: A demanding non-technical 6000er. The summit push starts near midnight; the air at the top makes every step a negotiation.
story: Stok Kangri is the kind of mountain that looks impossible from base camp and only slightly less so at 2 a.m. when you clip on the headtorch. There's nothing technical about it - and yet at 6,153 m the thin air turns simple walking into the hardest thing you've done.
We acclimatised for days in Leh and at advance base before the summit night. The reward is a sunrise over the Karakoram that I still think about on hard days at work.

gallery.caption: First light on the summit ridge

title: Roopkund
summary: The mystery lake trek - a glacial tarn rimmed with ancient skeletons, reached through meadows that turn gold in autumn.
story: Roopkund is famous for the human skeletons scattered around its shore, but what stays with you is the approach: endless golden meadows, the climb to Junargali, and the first sight of the frozen lake far below.
Autumn light on the Bugyals is unreal. This was the trek that made me fall for the high Himalaya.

title: Hampta Pass
summary: A dramatic crossover: lush green Kullu on one side, the stark cold desert of Spiti on the other, split by a single high pass.
story: Few treks change scenery as fast as Hampta. You start in pine forest and river meadows on the Kullu side and crest the pass into the moonscape of Spiti within a day.
The pass day is long and the descent is steep, but standing on the col with two completely different worlds on either side is hard to beat.

gallery.caption: River meadows on the Kullu side

title: Kashmir Great Lakes
summary: Seven alpine lakes strung across high passes. Easily the most scenic stretch I've walked - every campsite better than the last.
story: The Kashmir Great Lakes trek is a greatest-hits reel of alpine scenery: meadow after meadow, pass after pass, and a new turquoise lake waiting on the far side of each.
It's not the highest trek I've done, but it might be the most beautiful. I'd go back tomorrow.

title: Kedarkantha
summary: A winter classic. Snow-loaded pines, a frozen summit climb at dawn, and a 360-degree ring of Himalayan peaks at the top.
story: Kedarkantha is the trek that hooks people on winter mountaineering. Snow from the first day, a magical campsite in the pines, and a summit dawn with peaks in every direction.
Simple, accessible, and unforgettable - the perfect introduction to snow.

title: Sandakphu
summary: The 'Sleeping Buddha' ridge - four of the five highest peaks on earth, including Everest and Kanchenjunga, on one horizon.
story: From Sandakphu, on a clear morning, you can line up Everest, Lhotse, Makalu, and Kanchenjunga in a single sweep - the famous 'Sleeping Buddha' silhouette.
The trail rides the India-Nepal border through rhododendron forest and tiny ridgeline villages. Cold, windy, and absolutely worth it.
